Using a Monte Carlo simulation framework, we will compare model outputs for fire return intervals, fuel recovery times, and competitive outcomes under different climate scenarios, quantifying the divergence in predictions between the two trait representations.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, empirical test of when and where intraspecific trait variation is essential for accurate ecological modeling. It will directly inform the development of more robust DGVMs and fire prediction tools. The findings will advance the theoretical understanding of feedbacks in ecology and evolution by linking micro-evolutionary processes (trait variation) to macro-ecological patterns (biome distribution).
proposals.novelty: While intraspecific variation is widely acknowledged, its necessity—rather than just its existence—for functional models in fire-driven grassy ecosystems remains an untested assumption. This study moves beyond documenting variation to rigorously challenging a foundational modeling paradigm.

proposals.title: Disentangling the Causal Loop: Quantifying the Trait-to-Environment Pathway in Grassy Ecosystem Assembly
proposals.motivation: A central challenge in ecology is distinguishing whether plant traits are merely filtered by the environment or actively construct it, creating feedbacks that shape ecosystem states. In grassy systems, traits like bud position and dormancy influence fire spread and fuel loads, but correlational studies cannot establish causality. This limits our ability to predict ecosystem responses to climate change and management, as the direction of the dominant feedback remains unclear.
proposals.hypothesis: In grassy ecosystems, community-weighted mean traits (e.g., bud position, seed dormancy) exert a causal, quantifiable influence on subsequent environmental properties (e.g., fire return interval, soil carbon), and this trait-to-environment pathway can rival or exceed the strength of the classic environment-to-trait filtering pathway.
proposals.method: We will apply a structural equation modeling (SEM) framework to longitudinal plot data from global grassy biomes. The key causal pathway will be identified using lagged models, where environmental measurements post-date trait surveys. To address endogeneity, we will employ instrumental variable (IV) regression within the SEM, using spatially explicit instruments (e.g., historical climate stability, bedrock phosphorus) that are presumed to influence contemporary environment only via their legacy on trait assembly. Model comparisons will quantify the relative strength of the two opposing pathways.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorous, causal quantification of the trait-to-environment feedback in grassy ecosystems. It will advance the conceptual framework of ecological feedbacks from a theoretical construct to a testable, quantifiable model. The findings will identify specific biomes or conditions where biotic assembly is a primary driver of abiotic conditions, refining predictions of ecosystem resilience.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the explicit causal identification strategy, combining lagged data with instrumental variables in ecology to statistically isolate the trait-to-environment pathway, a direction of influence that is often assumed but rarely tested against its reverse.

proposals.title: Strategic Trait Collection for Environmental Diagnosis: An Inverse Modeling Framework for Grassy Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: In grassy ecosystems, plant functional traits are used to infer past and present environmental drivers like fire frequency and drought. However, trait collection is costly and often ad-hoc, limiting our ability to robustly reconstruct environmental histories from paleoecological or biogeographic data. A systematic method is needed to identify which missing trait measurements would most improve our power to diagnose environmental conditions from observed trait assemblages.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by inverting established trait-environment models, we can quantify the diagnostic value of specific, unmeasured traits and generate a prioritized 'demand map' for trait data collection that maximizes the predictive power for key environmental factors like fire return interval and growing season moisture stress.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian inverse modeling framework. First, we will compile existing global datasets linking grass and savanna woody plant traits (e.g., seed dormancy, bud protection, leaf economics) to environmental gradients. Using these, we will construct probabilistic forward models predicting traits from environment. We will then invert these models to compute the mutual information between potential new trait measurements and target environmental variables. This will allow us to rank traits by their expected reduction in posterior predictive uncertainty for environmental diagnosis across different global grassy biomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, model-driven strategy for prioritizing functional trait measurement in under-sampled regions. It will directly enhance the rigor of environmental reconstructions in paleoecology and improve monitoring of ecosystem responses to climate change in critical grassy biomes like those studied in Madagascar and beyond.
proposals.novelty: While trait-environment modeling is common, the explicit inversion of these relationships to guide future data collection for environmental diagnosis is a novel, synergistic approach that bridges predictive ecology and strategic sampling theory.

proposals.title: Trait Syndromes as Predictors of Fire Regime and Climate Feedbacks in Grassy Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Grassy ecosystem dynamics are governed by the feedbacks between fire, climate, and plant traits. Traditional trait-environment studies assume traits are filtered by the environment. However, if dominant trait combinations (e.g., bud location, dormancy, flammability) can, in aggregate, predict key environmental parameters like fire frequency and intensity, it would fundamentally shift our understanding of ecosystem self-organization and resilience in the face of global change.
proposals.hypothesis: That the spatial distribution of key plant functional traits (e.g., bud position, bark thickness, leaf dry matter content, seed dormancy) in grassy ecosystems can robustly predict local fire regime parameters (fire return interval, season, intensity) and microclimatic conditions, challenging the unidirectional assumption of environmental filtering.
proposals.method: We will compile a global dataset of plot-level plant trait data and paired environmental data (fire history from remote sensing, local climate, soil). Using a causal inference framework (e.g., Structural Equation Modelling) and machine learning algorithms (Random Forest or Gradient Boosting regressors), we will model fire regime and climate variables as dependent functions of community-weighted mean traits and trait diversity metrics. Model performance will be compared against null models where environment predicts traits.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel quantitative framework for testing the strength of trait-mediated environmental feedbacks. It will directly inform conservation by identifying which trait syndromes promote or suppress fire, offering a functional basis for managing ecosystem states under changing climates and fire regimes.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the classic trait-environment paradigm by treating aggregated traits as the predictor and key environmental drivers (fire, microclimate) as the response, formally testing a core but under-examined premise of ecological feedback theory.

proposals.title: Scaling from Pixels to Traits: Developing Ecological Tully-Fisher Relations for Grassy Ecosystem Function
proposals.motivation: Trait-based ecology is constrained by the difficulty of measuring key plant functional traits (e.g., leaf mass per area, nitrogen content, stem specific density) at landscape scales, particularly in extensive, heterogeneous grassy biomes. This limits our ability to model critical ecosystem processes like flammability, herbivory, and carbon cycling under global change. Remote sensing offers vast spatial and temporal coverage, but current trait retrieval methods often rely on complex, site-specific spectral models without a robust theoretical scaling framework.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that strong, generalizable scaling laws exist between foundational plant traits and integrative, remotely-sensed structural and phenological metrics, analogous to astrophysical mass-luminosity relations, allowing for the accurate prediction of community-weighted trait values across grassy ecosystems.
proposals.method: We will compile a global database of direct trait measurements for dominant grassy biome species, paired with co-located satellite data (Sentinel-2, Landsat). Using a hierarchical Bayesian modeling framework, we will statistically relate traits to scalable remote sensing proxies, including seasonal indices of greenness (NDVI), canopy structure (GEDI-derived plant area index), and phenological metrics (start/end of growing season). The models will be validated across independent sites in savannas and grasslands on different continents, testing for generality versus biome-specific calibration.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first generalized 'ecological scaling functions' for key traits, creating a practical toolbox to map plant functional diversity and community assembly rules at biome scales. It will directly enhance large-scale models of fire spread, herbivore carrying capacity, and ecosystem responses to climate change by providing spatially explicit trait inputs.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in explicitly adapting the conceptual framework of astrophysical scaling laws to ecology, moving beyond correlative spectral trait models to seek fundamental, physics-based relationships between plant form/function and observable integrative properties. It leverages the unique combination of newly available global trait databases and high-resolution remote sensing products pre-2026.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Adaptive Sampling to Optimize Trait Prediction in Grassy Biomes
proposals.motivation: Global trait databases are essential for understanding plant functional responses to fire and climate, but remain severely incomplete, especially for grassy ecosystems. This data gap limits our ability to model ecosystem dynamics and test evolutionary hypotheses about fire-adapted traits like dormancy and resprouting. Current gap-filling relies on random or convenience sampling, which is inefficient and fails to strategically reduce uncertainty in trait evolution models.
proposals.hypothesis: An adaptive sampling framework that prioritizes trait measurement for species with the highest phylogenetic prediction uncertainty will accelerate the reduction of global trait imputation error and improve the power to detect evolutionary correlations between fire-related traits.
proposals.method: We will develop an R-based framework integrating phylogenetic generalized least squares (PGLS) and Bayesian phylogenetic models to predict missing trait values (e.g., seed dormancy depth, bud protection) and estimate prediction variance across a phylogeny of grassy biome species. Using optimization algorithms (e.g., a greedy algorithm or simulated annealing), we will iteratively identify which missing species-trait combination, if measured, would maximally reduce the total phylogenetic prediction variance across the tree. This creates a ranked sampling protocol for field and lab work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, actionable protocol for targeted trait data collection that maximizes informational return per sampling effort. It will directly enhance global trait databases like TRY, enabling more robust tests of evolutionary feedbacks between fire, climate, and plant strategies in grassy ecosystems.
proposals.novelty: While phylogenetic imputation exists, directly using its uncertainty estimates to guide future data collection in an adaptive, optimization-based loop is a novel fusion of comparative biology and optimal experimental design, moving beyond passive gap-filling to active, hypothesis-driven sampling.

proposals.title: Inferring fire-adaptive trait variation in grasses: A hierarchical Bayesian inverse model linking community assembly to individual performance
proposals.motivation: Predicting grassy ecosystem responses to climate and fire regime changes requires understanding intraspecific trait variation (ITV), especially in key functional traits like bud bank depth, dormancy, and flammability. However, exhaustive trait measurements across environmental gradients are logistically impossible for vast, biodiverse systems like savannas. This data paucity severely limits mechanistic models of community assembly and resilience.
proposals.hypothesis: The critical parameters governing intraspecific variation in fire-adaptive traits can be robustly inferred by inverting community assembly models, using observed species abundances and sparse trait data across environmental gradients.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian state-space model. The core integrates a species performance sub-model, based on known physiological tolerances (e.g., to fire-return interval), with a community assembly sub-model using a maximum entropy (MaxEnt) framework. The model will be implemented in Stan, using Hamiltonian Monte Carlo for inference. It will be trained and validated on pre-existing datasets from Bond's work (e.g., Malagasy grasslands, global fire spread limits) containing species composition, climate, fire history, and sparse trait measurements.
proposals.contribution: This will provide the first practical framework to quantify ITV in data-poor grassy ecosystems, directly informing models of biome stability. It will generate testable, species-specific predictions of trait-environment relationships, advancing the integration of physiological ecology and community assembly theory.
proposals.novelty: The approach inverts the classic trait-to-community prediction problem, using community patterns as data to infer trait variation parameters, bypassing fatal data requirements. It formally links species-level physiological performance theory with macro-ecological patterns in a statistically rigorous, hierarchical framework.

proposals.title: Integrating Intraspecific Trait Plasticity into Dynamic Global Vegetation Models: A Focus on Fire-Fuel Feedbacks in Grassy Biomes
proposals.motivation: Dynamic Global Vegetation Models (DGVMs) are crucial for predicting ecosystem responses to climate change, but they typically represent plant species with fixed trait values, ignoring continuous intraspecific variation (ITV). This is a critical limitation for modeling grassy ecosystems, where fire-fuel feedbacks are strongly mediated by traits like leaf dry matter content and stem specific density, which vary continuously along environmental gradients. Ignoring this plasticity can lead to inaccurate predictions of biome boundaries, fire regimes, and carbon cycling under future climates.
proposals.hypothesis: That incorporating continuous intraspecific trait variation, driven by trait-environment relationships for key grass and savanna woody species, will significantly improve the predictive accuracy of DGVMs for fire-prone grassy ecosystems, particularly in simulating biome distributions and fire frequency.
proposals.method: We will develop a hierarchical Bayesian trait distribution model, parameterized using existing global trait databases (e.g., TRY) and species occurrence data, to predict continuous trait values as a function of climate, soil, and fire history. This model will be integrated into the LPJ-GUESS DGVM by replacing fixed species-trait parameters with probability distributions. The fire module will be explicitly linked to these dynamic traits (e.g., moisture of extinction, fuel bulk density). Model performance will be tested against observed biome maps and remote-sensing-derived fire return intervals across global savanna and grassland gradients.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, mechanistic framework for incorporating ITV into large-scale ecosystem models, moving beyond the species-as-fixed-entity paradigm. It will directly improve projections of grassy biome resilience and transitions under climate change, offering critical insights for conservation, as highlighted in Bond's work on Madagascar and fire spread limits. The resulting open-source model code will be a tool for the broader community.
proposals.novelty: While trait-based ecology recognizes ITV, its integration into process-based DGVMs via continuous probabilistic functions, specifically to mechanistically couple trait plasticity with fire feedbacks, represents a significant advance over previous modeling efforts that use fixed traits or discrete plant functional types.

proposals.title: Fire, Frost, and Function: A Trait-Based Framework for Parameterizing Grassland Dynamics in Earth System Models
proposals.motivation: Earth system models (ESMs) struggle to accurately simulate the dynamics of grassy biomes, which cover ~40% of the terrestrial surface and are critical to global carbon, water, and fire cycles. A key limitation is the poor representation of plant functional diversity, particularly traits governing responses to fire and climate extremes like frost, which are abstracted into overly simplistic parameters. This gap hinders predictions of biome shifts and carbon feedbacks under climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a core set of measurable plant traits—specifically bud bank size and depth, bark thickness, and seed dormancy duration—can be directly linked to key ESM parameters for mortality, recovery, and competition, thereby improving the simulation of grassland resilience to fire and climate variability.
proposals.method: We will compile a new global database for these traits from published studies, herbarium records, and targeted field sampling in key grassy ecosystems (e.g., Madagascar, Cerrado, African savannas). Using hierarchical Bayesian models, we will quantify the relationships between these traits and observed post-disturbance recovery rates. These statistical models will be used to develop transfer functions that map trait values to demographic rates (e.g., fire-induced mortality, resprouting probability) for Dynamic Global Vegetation Model (DGVM) components within ESMs like CLM and JULES.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a rigorously parameterized, trait-driven module for grass and savanna tree dynamics, directly enhancing the functional ecology within ESMs. It will deliver a prioritized list of the most influential traits for model improvement and a publicly available trait database, providing a roadmap for future data collection to reduce model uncertainty.
proposals.novelty: While trait databases exist, this approach is novel in its exclusive focus on traits that are both measurable in the field and mechanistically relevant to the specific processes (fire mortality, resprouting, dormancy) that ESMs must simulate, moving beyond correlative leaf-economic traits to whole-plant demographic strategies.

proposals.title: Phylogenetic Priors and Remote Sensing Data Fusion for Global Imputation of Fire-Related Plant Traits in Grassy Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Understanding the functional biogeography of grassy ecosystems, which are extensive, fire-prone, and critical for global carbon dynamics, is limited by the scarcity of species-level trait data across continents. Remote sensing provides continuous spatial data but lacks taxonomic specificity, while phylogenetic comparative methods capture evolutionary trait constraints but lack spatial explicitness. Bridging this gap is essential for scaling plot-based ecology to predict ecosystem responses to climate and fire regime changes.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical Bayesian model that uses phylogenetic relatedness to structure prior distributions for plant functional traits will significantly improve the accuracy of species-level trait imputation from continuous remote sensing data compared to models ignoring evolutionary history.
proposals.method: We will develop a Bayesian hierarchical model where the prior for a species' trait value is a multivariate normal distribution conditioned on its phylogenetic position (using a Brownian motion or Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process). This phylogenetically-structured prior will be updated with likelihoods derived from remote sensing covariates (e.g., Sentinel-2 reflectance spectra, GEDI LiDAR metrics) using a flexible machine learning sub-model (e.g., Gaussian Process regression). The model will be trained and validated using existing global trait databases (TRY), phylogenies (GBOTB), and remote sensing data, with a focus on fire-related traits like leaf dry matter content and stem specific density.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first continuous, phylogenetically-informed global maps of key plant traits for grassy biomes, directly applicable to dynamic global vegetation models. It will provide a novel statistical framework for fusing evolutionary biology and remote sensing ecology, enabling tests of how phylogenetic niche conservatism structures functional biogeography at continental scales.
proposals.novelty: While phylogenetic imputation and remote sensing trait retrieval exist separately, their fusion into a single Bayesian framework to create spatially explicit, species-aware trait maps is a novel synthesis. This approach moves beyond community-weighted means to probabilistically infer traits for unsampled species within a constrained evolutionary trait-space.

proposals.title: Unveiling the functional architecture of grassy ecosystems: A 'single-plot genomics' approach to trait-environment feedbacks
proposals.motivation: Grassy ecosystem dynamics are governed by feedbacks among fire, climate, and plant traits, but our understanding is limited by coarse-scale models that average over critical micro-variation. This averaging obscures the fundamental mechanisms of community assembly, resilience, and evolutionary adaptation, particularly for traits like dormancy and regeneration. A paradigm shift towards hyper-local measurement is needed to reveal the true nature of these ecological and evolutionary feedbacks.
proposals.hypothesis: By treating small, homogeneous plots as fundamental analytical units ('ecological cells'), we will reveal deterministic, fine-scale trait-environment relationships—particularly for regeneration traits like seed dormancy—that are invisible at landscape scales, fundamentally reshaping models of grassy ecosystem response to climate and fire.
proposals.method: We will establish a standardized pipeline deploying portable sensor arrays (hyperspectral reflectance, LiDAR, soil moisture/pH/conductivity probes) within 1m² plots in a South African savanna. For each 'cell', we will exhaustively measure abiotic conditions and key plant functional traits (e.g., SLA, leaf N, seed mass, dormancy class via germination trials). We will use Bayesian hierarchical models and Gaussian Process regressions to build hyper-local trait-environment models, explicitly testing for scale-dependency by comparing 'single-plot' models to aggregated, landscape-scale models.
proposals.contribution: This will produce the first high-resolution 'trait-environment atlas' for a grassy ecosystem, directly testing the scale-dependency of classic ecological relationships. It will provide a novel methodological framework for studying ecological feedbacks and generate mechanistic insights into how micro-environmental heterogeneity drives patterns of dormancy, regeneration, and resilience to fire and climate change.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its conceptual borrowing from single-cell genomics to treat plots as discrete functional units, and in its technical integration of portable, multi-sensor arrays to achieve exhaustive, simultaneous characterization of biotic and abiotic factors at a sub-meter scale rarely attempted in field ecology.

proposals.title: Microsite Determinants of Grass Functional Traits: Resolving Fire-Climate-Feedback Dynamics at the Plot Scale
proposals.motivation: Grassy ecosystem dynamics, central to Bond's work, are governed by fire-climate-vegetation feedbacks, but predictive trait-environment models often fail at local scales where these feedbacks operate. This mismatch limits our ability to forecast ecosystem responses to climate change or altered fire regimes, as broad-scale models smooth over critical micro-variation in soil, topography, and disturbance history that drive trait selection and species coexistence.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that within a single grassy biome, hyper-local environmental filters—specifically, fine-scale gradients in soil moisture, nutrient availability, and fire return interval—explain a greater proportion of variance in key plant functional traits (e.g., specific leaf area, seed dormancy, bud protection) than regional climate variables.
proposals.method: We will establish a nested sampling design across a fire frequency gradient in a South African savanna. In 50 plots (10x10m), we will collect hyper-local data on soil properties, micro-topography, and fuel loads. For all perennial grass and forb species, we will measure key functional traits and quantify seed bank dormancy status. Trait-environment relationships will be modeled using Bayesian Hierarchical Models (BHMs), which explicitly partition variance across spatial scales, and Gradient Boosting Machines (e.g., XGBoost) to identify non-linear interactions and key hyper-local predictors.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first quantitatively rigorous, micro-scale trait-environment models for grassy ecosystems, directly informing how fire management alters local community assembly. It will advance the theory of ecological feedbacks by pinpointing the environmental thresholds at which fire spread or plant traits shift, providing a mechanistic template for scaling from plots to landscapes.
proposals.novelty: While trait-based ecology is well-established, applying high-resolution statistical learning to model traits at the plot scale within the explicit context of fire-climate feedbacks is novel. This moves beyond describing community-weighted means to predicting trait values from micro-habitat conditions, a key step for mechanistic forecasting.

proposals.title: Integrating microbial and herbivore functional traits into the TRY framework to predict fire-vegetation feedbacks in grassy ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the TRY Plant Trait Database has revolutionized the study of plant functional ecology and its links to ecosystem processes like fire regimes. However, critical biotic feedbacks involving soil microbes (mediating nutrient cycling and plant growth) and herbivores (mediating fuel load and structure) are poorly integrated into these trait-based models. This limits our ability to predict ecosystem responses to climate change, particularly in fire-prone grassy systems where plant-microbe-animal interactions are fundamental.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating key functional traits of soil microbes (e.g., growth rate, enzyme activity) and mammalian herbivores (e.g., body mass, dietary selectivity) with plant trait data will significantly improve the predictive power of models for fire behavior, fuel recovery, and vegetation state transitions in grassy ecosystems.
proposals.method: We will extend the TRY database schema to accommodate microbial (e.g., from literature and GenBank meta-analyses) and herbivore trait data (e.g., from EltonTraits, PanTHERIA). A hierarchical Bayesian modeling framework will be developed to link these multi-kingdom trait matrices to ecosystem properties (fuel load, soil N). We will apply this integrated model to case studies from global grassy biomes (e.g., savannas studied in Bond's prior work) to test predictions against empirical data on fire spread limits and vegetation dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first openly accessible, integrated trait database encompassing plants, soil microbes, and herbivores, directly serving the ecological community. It will deliver a novel modeling framework that quantifies the relative strength of plant, microbial, and animal traits in driving fire-vegetation feedbacks, moving beyond plant-centric perspectives.
proposals.novelty: While trait-based ecology is well-established for plants, its systematic integration with microbial and animal functional data within a unified, scalable database and modeling architecture is a major, non-trivial advancement. This bridges disciplines (microbial ecology, zoology, fire ecology) to tackle whole-ecosystem responses.

proposals.title: Optimizing Trait Sampling in Grassy Ecosystems: A Demand-Mapping Framework to Reduce Uncertainty in Local Fire-Vegetation Feedbacks
proposals.motivation: Understanding fine-scale trait-environment relationships, such as how seed dormancy and flammability traits mediate fire-vegetation feedbacks, is critical for predicting ecosystem responses to climate change. However, field campaigns to collect plant functional traits are resource-intensive, and current sampling strategies are often ad-hoc, leading to inefficient data collection that poorly constrains local ecological models. A systematic method to prioritize which traits to measure and where is needed to maximize the predictive power of limited sampling efforts in heterogeneous landscapes like savannas.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying global sensitivity analysis to an ensemble of hyper-local trait-environment models will identify specific, high-impact sampling locations where collecting missing trait data will most rapidly reduce uncertainty in predictions of key ecosystem processes, such as fire spread probability.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework that first builds an ensemble of plot-scale Bayesian structural equation models linking mapped environmental drivers (soil, climate, fire history) to key response variables (e.g., fuel load, fire return interval). For each model cell, we will use the Sobol method to perform variance-based sensitivity analysis, quantifying the contribution of each uncertain trait parameter to total output variance. This creates a 'demand score'—the expected reduction in prediction uncertainty per trait measurement—which is mapped spatially to generate dynamic sampling priorities for field campaigns.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, operational tool for designing efficient trait-sampling campaigns, directly applicable to conservation and fire management in grassy biomes. It will advance the field of functional ecology by creating a formal, iterative link between model uncertainty, field data collection, and the refinement of local ecological predictions.
proposals.novelty: While sensitivity analysis is used in models, its application to generate spatially-explicit, actionable 'demand maps' for guiding real-world field sampling represents a significant methodological innovation. This moves beyond post-hoc gap analysis to a proactive, optimization-based framework for ecological data acquisition.

proposals.title: A Statistical Mechanics of Vegetation: Scaling from Plant Trait Distributions to Ecosystem Carbon and Water Fluxes in Grassy Biomes
proposals.motivation: Scaling from plant traits to ecosystem-level fluxes of carbon and water remains a central challenge in ecology, limiting our ability to predict biome responses to climate change. Current approaches often rely on simplistic trait means or plant functional types, neglecting the critical role of trait variation and covariance within communities. This gap is particularly acute in grassy ecosystems, where fire, herbivory, and climate interact to shape highly diverse trait distributions.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that ecosystem-scale gas exchange can be accurately predicted as the statistical expectation of an individual-level flux model, integrated over the empirically measured joint probability distribution of key plant traits (e.g., specific leaf area, leaf nitrogen, rooting depth) across the community.
proposals.method: We will develop the framework using data from global grassy ecosystem sites (e.g., eddy covariance flux towers). First, we will define a mechanistic, individual-plant flux model for photosynthesis and transpiration as our constitutive equation. We will then intensively sample the joint distribution of relevant traits (using seed and trait databases) at these sites. The scaling operator will be implemented computationally: the ecosystem flux is calculated as the integral of the individual flux function over the multidimensional trait distribution, weighted by plant density, using numerical integration (e.g., Monte Carlo methods) to handle covariance structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, first-principles scaling method that explicitly incorporates biodiversity (trait diversity and covariance) into predictions of ecosystem function. It will yield a novel, testable framework for understanding how changes in community composition, driven by fire or climate, alter biome-scale fluxes, directly informing dynamic global vegetation models.
proposals.novelty: The approach is novel in its direct conceptual and mathematical analogy to statistical mechanics, treating trait distributions as the fundamental state variables for scaling, rather than relying on ad-hoc aggregation rules or predefined functional types common in pre-2026 ecology.

proposals.title: Facilitative Overrides: Quantifying Tree-Grass Positive Interactions and Their Role in Savanna Resilience
proposals.motivation: Savanna ecology has long been dominated by the paradigm of tree-grass competition, a cornerstone of models like Bond's (2008) demographic bottleneck framework. However, pre-2026 field observations, particularly in mesic or degraded systems, suggest trees can facilitate grass growth via microclimate amelioration, nutrient cycling, and hydraulic lift. This proposal challenges the foundational competition assumption to explore how and when facilitation becomes a critical driver of ecosystem structure, function, and resilience to climate change.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in certain environmental contexts (e.g., high rainfall seasonality, nutrient-poor soils, or post-disturbance recovery), tree canopies facilitate grass layer productivity and persistence, creating a positive feedback that enhances overall ecosystem resilience to drought and fire.
proposals.method: We will conduct a global meta-analysis of pre-2026 studies measuring grass biomass, composition, and seed bank dynamics under vs. away from tree canopies. Concurrently, we will establish field experiments in selected Malagasy grassy ecosystems, using paired plots under isolated trees and in open grassland. We will measure soil moisture (TDR probes), light (PAR sensors), grass productivity (clip plots), and grass species composition, with a focus on seed dormancy and recruitment traits. Data will be analyzed using structural equation modeling (SEM) to quantify the pathways and strength of tree effects on grass layers.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, mechanistic framework for tree-grass facilitation, directly challenging and expanding classic competition models. It will identify the environmental thresholds where facilitation overrides competition, offering new insights for restoration ecology, particularly in predicting savanna responses to woody encroachment and climate variability.
proposals.novelty: While competition is well-studied, systematically inverting this model to test for widespread, context-dependent facilitation as a stabilizing mechanism in grassy ecosystems is a novel synthesis. It integrates Bond's expertise in fire and evolution with niche construction theory, moving beyond a binary competitive view.

proposals.title: Mapping the Grass-Fire Feedback: A Remote Sensing Framework for Parameterizing Fuel Moisture and Biomass Dynamics in Grassy Ecosystems
proposals.motivation: Grassy ecosystems are globally significant carbon stores and biodiversity hotspots, regulated by the grass-fire feedback where fire promotes grasses and grasses fuel fires. Quantifying this feedback at landscape scales remains a major challenge, limiting our ability to model ecosystem responses to climate change and altered fire regimes. Pre-2026 remote sensing offers unprecedented, spatially explicit data on fuel properties, but these have not been systematically integrated to parameterize the feedback's environmental limits.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that satellite-derived maps of live fuel moisture content (LFMC) and aboveground biomass can be combined to create a dynamic fuel metric that accurately predicts the probability and spatial extent of fire spread in contrasting grassy ecosystems, thereby quantifying the strength of the grass-fire feedback.
proposals.method: We will use MODIS and Sentinel-2 data to generate time-series maps of LFMC via radiative transfer models (e.g., PROSAIL) and aboveground biomass using SAR data (Sentinel-1) and vegetation indices. These will be integrated into a fuel availability index. We will apply machine learning (Random Forest or Gradient Boosting) to relate this index, alongside climate and topography, to historical fire perimeters from MODIS/ VIIRS active fire data across study regions in Madagascar and southern Africa. Model performance will be validated against independent fire datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first landscape-scale, remotely sensed parameterization of the grass-fire feedback, directly linking fuel dynamics to fire spread outcomes. It will advance ecological theory on fire-environment limits and provide a transferable framework for improving dynamic global vegetation models under changing climates.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the concurrent, operational use of satellite-derived LFMC and biomass to create a dynamic fuel variable for fire prediction, moving beyond static fuel maps or meteorological proxies, specifically to test Bond's theories on environmental limits to fire spread in grassy systems.

proposals.title: Inverting Savanna Dynamics: A Computational Framework to Solve for Fire and Herbivore Regimes Required for Target Tree-Grass Ratios
proposals.motivation: Savanna management and restoration often aim for specific tree-grass ratios to balance biodiversity, carbon storage, and ecosystem services. However, current models are largely descriptive or predictive, simulating outcomes from given fire and herbivory regimes. This creates a trial-and-error approach to management. A formal inverse solution is needed to efficiently determine the precise combinations of disturbance needed to achieve conservation and land-use targets.
proposals.hypothesis: That the well-established, pre-2026 understanding of grass-tree competition, fire spread limits, and consumer-resource dynamics can be mathematically inverted to yield a feasible set of fire return intervals and herbivore densities for any ecologically plausible target tree-grass ratio.
proposals.method: We will develop an inverse modeling framework by first formulating a parsimonious process-based model of savanna dynamics, integrating Bond's work on fire spread limits and grass fuel dynamics with consumer (herbivore) functional responses. This forward model will be embedded within a Bayesian inverse problem solver. Using Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling, we will invert the model to identify the joint posterior distributions of fire return interval and herbivore density parameters that are consistent with a user-specified tree-grass ratio target, given quantified environmental constraints (e.g., rainfall, soil).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first general computational tool for solving the inverse management problem in grassy ecosystems. It will shift the paradigm from predicting outcomes of disturbances to prescribing disturbances for outcomes. The framework will deliver not just single solutions but feasible ranges and trade-offs between fire and herbivory, directly informing adaptive management and restoration policy.
proposals.novelty: While forward models of savanna dynamics are common, explicitly inverting them to solve for required management inputs is a novel, non-obvious application of inverse problem theory to ecosystem management. It moves beyond correlation to a mechanistic, solution-oriented framework.

ideas.title: Phylogenetically-Informed Remote Sensing for Global Plant Trait Imputation
ideas.description: This research direction develops a hierarchical Bayesian framework that fuses phylogenetic comparative methods with remote sensing ecology. The model uses a known plant phylogeny to structure priors for the trait values of related species, creating a constrained trait-space. Continuous but low-specificity remote sensing signals (e.g., canopy reflectance, LiDAR metrics) are then integrated as data, allowing the model to probabilistically impute detailed, species-level traits (e.g., leaf mass per area, wood density) across geographic space. This enables the creation of continuous, phylogenetically-informed trait maps without direct field measurements for every species, revolutionizing the scaling of functional ecology from plots to continents.

ideas.title: From Molecular Collisions to Bulk Properties: A Statistical Mechanics Framework for Scaling Plant Traits to Ecosystem Fluxes
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core solution from statistical mechanics to the problem of scaling plant traits to ecosystem fluxes. The analogous problem—deriving bulk gas properties from unpredictable molecular motions—was solved by shifting focus from individual particles to the statistical distribution of their states and averaging their contributions. Here, we propose to develop a framework where measurable individual plant traits (the microstates) are characterized by a joint probability distribution across the ecosystem. A mechanistic flux model for a 'mean individual' serves as the constitutive equation linking trait values to instantaneous gas/energy exchange. The emergent ecosystem flux is then the statistical expectation—the integral of this individual-level flux function over the multidimensional trait distribution, scaled by total abundance. This approach directly links individual trait measurements to ecosystem fluxes through a rigorous scaling operator defined by the trait distribution, bypassing simplistic aggregation and explicitly accounting for trait diversity and covariance.

ideas.title: Causal Disentanglement of Trait-Environment Feedback Using Lagged and Instrumental Variable Models
ideas.description: This research program rigorously tests for a causal pathway from community trait distributions to subsequent environmental properties, moving beyond correlation. It utilizes longitudinal datasets where environmental measurements are taken after trait surveys, and employs instrumental variables (e.g., historical climate refugia, bedrock geology) that influence traits but affect contemporary environment only through those traits. The analysis explicitly models and compares the strength of the trait-to-environment pathway against the environment-to-trait pathway within a unified structural equation model, aiming to identify ecosystems where biotic assembly is a dominant predictor of abiotic conditions.

ideas.title: A Mechanistic, Inverse Modeling Framework to Infer Intraspecific Trait Variation from Sparse Data and Community Patterns
ideas.description: This research develops a novel modeling paradigm that overcomes the fatal data requirements of traditional approaches by inverting the problem. Instead of requiring exhaustive trait measurements to predict communities, it uses observed community composition and sparse trait samples to infer the critical parameters of intraspecific trait variation and its environmental sensitivity. The core is a hierarchical Bayesian model that integrates species-specific physiological performance theory with community assembly rules, allowing information to flow from well-studied model species to data-poor ones and from community-level patterns to individual trait distributions. This results in a practical, empirically-constrained framework that makes robust predictions about trait-mediated responses to environmental change even with incomplete data.

ideas.title: Inferring Plant Traits via Ecological Scaling Laws and Remote Sensing
ideas.description: This research idea challenges the assumption that direct, individual plant measurements are necessary for trait ecology by adapting a solution from astrophysics. In cosmology, scaling laws like the Tully-Fisher relation allow astronomers to infer fundamental properties (mass, distance) of galaxies from simple photometric observations, bypassing impossible individual measurements. We propose to develop analogous 'ecological scaling laws' by statistically linking foundational direct trait measurements to scalable remote sensing proxies (e.g., specific spectral bands, canopy structure metrics, phenological categories). This will create a framework to reliably infer continuous plant traits (physiological, structural, chemical) across populations and ecosystems using only categorical or remote sensing data, dramatically expanding the scale of trait-based studies.

ideas.title: Inverse Trait-Environment Mapping to Prioritize Data for Predictive Environmental Diagnosis
ideas.description: This research fuses the concepts by developing a method to identify which specific, currently missing trait measurements would most powerfully invert the classic trait-environment relationship. Instead of just reducing uncertainty in models that predict traits from environment, the goal is to pinpoint the trait data needed to build robust models that predict environmental factors—like stress regimes, disturbance history, or microclimate—from observed trait distributions. This creates a targeted 'demand map' for trait data based on its value for environmental diagnosis and reconstruction. The synergy enables a strategic, model-guided approach to trait collection that maximizes its utility for answering inverse questions in biogeography, paleoecology, and ecosystem monitoring.

ideas.title: Phylogenetically-informed adaptive sampling for optimal plant trait gap-filling
ideas.description: This research develops an adaptive framework that integrates phylogenetic comparative methods directly into the data gap identification and prioritization process. The system uses phylogenetic models to generate probabilistic predictions and, crucially, estimates of prediction uncertainty for missing trait data across the tree of life. It then employs optimization algorithms to prioritize which species-trait combinations to measure next, balancing the reduction of global prediction uncertainty with the testing of specific evolutionary hypotheses. This creates a cost-effective, iterative sampling strategy that systematically fills the most informative gaps, accelerating the construction of a complete and evolutionarily insightful global trait database.

ideas.title: Hyper-Local Trait-Environment Models via Single-Cell Genomics-Inspired Sampling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from single-cell genomics, where moving from bulk tissue analysis to profiling individual cells revealed critical functional heterogeneity. The analogous ecological problem is that regional or landscape-scale trait-environment models average out crucial micro-variation. The adapted solution is to build hyper-local models by treating individual plots or microhabitats as fundamental units, analogous to cells. This involves creating a standardized pipeline for 'single-plot genomics': using portable, high-resolution sensor arrays to exhaustively capture plant functional traits and abiotic conditions (light, soil moisture, nutrients) at the scale of a few square meters. The resulting 'ecological cell' data would be used to build ultra-high-resolution trait-environment relationship models, revealing patterns and processes invisible at coarser scales, much as single-cell atlases transformed understanding of tissue function and disease.

ideas.title: Hyper-local Trait Demand Mapping for Targeted Field Sampling
ideas.description: This research direction develops a framework to generate high-resolution 'demand maps' that pinpoint specific micro-habitats or plots where missing trait data would most effectively reduce uncertainty in hyper-local trait-environment models. By applying sensitivity analysis to an ensemble of plot-scale models, the method quantifies the expected uncertainty reduction per unit of sampling effort for each missing trait at each location. This allows for the creation of dynamic, spatially-explicit sampling priorities that guide field campaigns to collect the most impactful data first. The resulting iterative loop—where new, targeted trait measurements immediately refine local models and update the demand map—optimizes resource allocation to rapidly converge on accurate, fine-scale ecological predictions.

ideas.title: Adapting Gaussian Process Regression from Geostatistics to Model Continuous Intraspecific Trait Variation
ideas.description: This research idea proposes to improve the modeling of continuous intraspecific trait variation by drawing an analogy from geostatistics. In that field, Gaussian Process Regression (Griging) is used to model and predict continuous spatial phenomena from sparse data points by defining covariance based on geographic distance. The analogous solution is adapted by treating the 'environmental niche' as the domain instead of physical space. We develop a modeling framework where an individual's trait value is modeled as a realization of a Gaussian process over multi-dimensional environmental axes. The kernel function of the process defines how trait similarity decays with 'environmental distance,' allowing for the interpolation and prediction of trait distributions across continuous environmental gradients. This approach directly incorporates trait-environment relationships, provides a probabilistic estimate of trait values at any environmental combination, and quantifies prediction uncertainty, offering a significant advancement over methods that bin environments or treat intraspecific variation as simple, discrete classes.

ideas.title: Use stable isotope ecology for water-use efficiency

ideas.title: Apply geostatistical kriging models to phylogenetic trait prediction
ideas.description: This idea adapts the geostatistical method of kriging to the problem of predicting trait values for unmeasured species. In geostatistics, kriging predicts values at unmeasured spatial locations by modeling spatial autocorrelation via a variogram. The analogous solution is to treat the phylogenetic tree as a 'trait space' where evolutionary distance (branch length) substitutes for spatial distance. A phylogenetic variogram would model how trait covariance decays with evolutionary divergence. This covariance structure is then used within a prediction framework (like generalized least squares or a Gaussian Process) to generate Best Linear Unbiased Predictions for missing species' traits, complete with measures of prediction uncertainty, thereby using the phylogenetic model directly to inform the priors and predictions for related, unmeasured species.

ideas.title: Discriminatory Trait Demand Mapping for Model Selection and Uncertainty Partitioning
ideas.description: This research creates a trait data priority map that targets not only parameter uncertainty but also structural uncertainty in ecological models. The system employs an ensemble of alternative model structures representing competing hypotheses about key ecological processes. For each model, sensitivity to missing traits is calculated, but the final demand score for a trait is weighted by its power to reduce overall ensemble variance and discriminate between structurally different models. This ensures prioritized trait data collection will most efficiently resolve fundamental theoretical disagreements and improve model fidelity, rather than just refining parameters within a potentially flawed paradigm.

ideas.title: A Dynamic, Self-Improving Trait Observatory via Citizen Science and Active Learning
ideas.description: This research establishes a dynamic observatory that integrates crowdsourced plant trait observations with machine learning for trait imputation. The core innovation is an active learning framework where the ML model, trained on existing data, identifies geographic and taxonomic gaps where new observations would most reduce prediction uncertainty. The citizen science platform then prioritizes these gaps by directing participants to collect specific traits for targeted species in their region. This creates a virtuous cycle: each new batch of crowdsourced data refines the model, which in turn generates more precise collection priorities, systematically and efficiently filling the global trait data matrix.

ideas.title: Phylogenetically-informed priors with integrated tree uncertainty
ideas.description: This method develops a hierarchical Bayesian framework that co-estimates the phylogeny and the model of trait evolution. Instead of using a fixed tree, the system samples from the posterior distribution of plausible phylogenies (accounting for topological and branch length uncertainty) and the posterior distribution of trait model parameters. This joint inference propagates phylogenetic uncertainty directly into the priors generated for missing species, producing robust predictions that are not contingent on a single potentially incorrect tree structure. The output is a full posterior predictive distribution for each unmeasured species, accurately reflecting both evolutionary and phylogenetic uncertainty.

ideas.title: Adapting Galaxy Zoo's classification interface for standardized plant trait crowdsourcing
ideas.description: This idea adapts a proven citizen science solution from astronomy—the Galaxy Zoo classification platform—to the problem of crowdsourcing standardized plant trait data. Galaxy Zoo successfully enabled millions of volunteers to perform complex morphological classifications via a guided, decision-tree interface that ensures data consistency. By applying this model, we can develop a specialized platform where participants upload plant photos and are led through a series of image-based, multiple-choice prompts to record specific functional traits (e.g., leaf size, stem thickness, reproductive structures). This structured approach minimizes observer variation, transforms trait measurement into an engaging task, and generates large, usable datasets for plant ecology and evolution research.

ideas.title: Federated Inverse Trait-Environment Modeling (FITEM)
ideas.description: This research fuses federated database architectures with inverse modeling to develop a distributed machine learning framework. The system trains models to predict environmental conditions from trait assemblages by performing computations locally across multiple, independent data repositories (e.g., herbaria, research networks, national databases) and only sharing model updates. This approach overcomes the major barriers of data centralization—such as sovereignty, sensitivity, and heterogeneity—while leveraging globally dispersed trait observations. The result is a collaboratively learned, robust inverse model capable of inferring past and present environmental gradients from trait data at a scale previously impossible.

ideas.title: Hierarchical Bayesian Networks for Robust Hyper-Local Trait-Environment Inference
ideas.description: This approach constructs a multi-level model where the trait-environment relationship at each plot is a unique instance drawn from a community-wide distribution. By fitting the global and local parameters simultaneously within a Bayesian framework, the system quantifies uncertainty for each plot-level estimate and identifies which plots exhibit relationships significantly different from the community mean. This creates a robust map of micro-variation that distinguishes reproducible local adaptations from statistical artifacts, enabling scalable inference from sparse plot-level data.

ideas.title: Implement a Semantic Mediation Layer for Cross-Domain Trait Data Querying
ideas.description: This idea adapts semantic data federation techniques from bioinformatics to solve the problem of querying trait data across distributed, heterogeneous sources in ecology, agriculture, and related life sciences. In bioinformatics, projects like Bio2RDF use shared ontologies and semantic web standards (RDF, SPARQL) to create a virtual integration layer, allowing queries to be mediated across databases like UniProt and KEGG without centralizing the data. The adapted research idea involves developing a similar semantic mediation layer for trait data. This system would leverage and extend existing trait ontologies to provide a common conceptual framework, enabling users to issue unified queries. Specialized mediation software would then translate these queries, fetch data from distributed sources (e.g., genomic databases, ecological field repositories, crop trial data stores), and integrate the results based on semantic meaning rather than just syntactic matching, thereby enabling powerful federated queries without the need for physical data centralization.

ideas.title: Causal Trait-Environment Inference: Resolving Non-Uniqueness with Generative Models and Uncertainty Quantification
ideas.description: This research develops a hybrid framework to robustly infer environmental conditions from trait data by directly confronting the non-uniqueness of the inverse problem. It combines process-based evolutionary and ecological simulation models to generate a comprehensive library of possible trait-environment pathways, encoding causal relationships. A machine learning model, trained on this synthetic library, then performs Bayesian inference on observed trait assemblages, outputting a constrained set of plausible environmental scenarios with explicit probabilities. This approach moves beyond single-point predictions to deliver actionable, uncertainty-aware reconstructions of past and present environmental filters.

ideas.title: Federated Learning for Privacy-Preserving Trait Imputation Across Distributed Databases
ideas.description: This research direction combines federated database architectures with machine learning for trait imputation to create a federated learning system for ecological and biodiversity data. Instead of centralizing data, the system distributes a global imputation model to local nodes (e.g., herbaria, gene banks, research institutes), where it is trained on local, gap-filled data. Only model parameter updates, not the raw data, are shared and aggregated to iteratively improve the global model. This allows for the creation of a comprehensive, high-quality predictive model that leverages all available data across a federation while strictly preserving data privacy, sovereignty, and access controls inherent to each distributed source.

ideas.title: Use Graph Neural Networks for trait imputation by modeling species as nodes in an ecological network
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from Social Network Analysis and Recommender Systems, where Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) are used to predict missing user attributes by leveraging relational network structure. The analogous solution involves modeling entities as interconnected nodes and using message-passing algorithms to infer missing data from neighbors. Adapted to trait imputation, we construct an ecological graph with species as nodes, connected by edges based on phylogenetic distance, habitat co-occurrence, or interaction data. A GNN model is then trained to propagate known trait information across this network, learning to predict missing trait values for a target species based on the aggregated features of its network neighbors. This method explicitly incorporates the complex relational structure of ecological communities, offering a powerful alternative to traditional imputation models that may not capture these interdependencies.

ideas.title: A Hierarchical Bayesian Framework for Trait Databases: Borrowing Strength from Local Clusters to Inform Global Estimates
ideas.description: This research seed challenges the assumption that a global trait database can adequately represent hyper-local adaptation. Drawing an analogy from pharmacology's solution to multi-regional clinical trials—where drug effects must be estimated globally across genetically and environmentally diverse populations—we propose adapting its hierarchical Bayesian statistical framework. In this adapted approach, individual studies or sampled populations serve as local nodes within a global trait model. The framework estimates trait values for each node, but these estimates are partially pooled ('borrow strength') from similar nodes and a global distribution. This creates a dynamic global database that does not erase local variation but instead explicitly models it, providing both global summaries and localized predictions with quantified uncertainty. The outcome is a database that acknowledges its own limitations in representativeness for any specific location while rigorously using all available data to improve inference at all scales.

ideas.title: A smartphone-integrated platform with embedded sensor-based validation for high-fidelity plant trait crowdsourcing
ideas.description: This system transforms a citizen scientist's smartphone into a guided, validated data collection tool for plant traits. Using the device's camera and inertial sensors, the app provides real-time feedback—such as confirming a leaf is in focus for area measurement or ensuring a ruler is properly aligned—to enforce standardized protocols. Submitted observations are accompanied by a quality score generated from sensor metadata and computer vision checks. This creates a tiered dataset where researchers can filter for observations that meet a defined quality threshold, ensuring robust, research-grade trait data from a crowdsourced network.

ideas.title: A Coordinated, Model-Informed Trait Observation Network for Earth System Models
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from high-energy physics, where detector design is driven by the specific data needs of theoretical models. The analogous problem is efficiently collecting experimental data to constrain fundamental models. The adapted solution is to create a coordinated, model-informed trait observation network for Earth System Models. A formal process would be established where modeling consortia identify priority traits—those to which model outputs are most sensitive and for which uncertainty is highest. This 'requirements document' would then guide the design of targeted observational systems, such as specialized sensor arrays on towers, drones, or satellites, and structured field campaigns. This ensures trait data collection is strategic, directly reducing key parametric uncertainties and validating process representations, thereby accelerating ESM development and improvement with maximum efficiency.

ideas.title: A Federated Pipeline for Trait Data Ingestion Using a Common Data Model
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from clinical research informatics, where the OMOP Common Data Model solves the problem of integrating heterogeneous electronic health records. The analogous solution involves defining a universal schema (the CDM) and creating source-specific adapters to transform local data into this standard format for centralized aggregation. Adapted to trait data mobilization, the research would involve: 1) Developing a Plant Trait Common Data Model (PT-CDM) with standardized semantics for traits, methods, taxa, and provenance. 2) Creating a library of open-source 'ETL adapter' tools tailored for major data sources (e.g., a script for specific herbarium database schemas, a tool for parsing trait tables from PDFs, a template for unpublished spreadsheets). This federated approach allows data holders to perform the complex mapping and quality checks locally using familiar tools, then submit perfectly formatted PT-CDM files to TRY, enabling efficient, scalable, and high-quality integration of data from literature, herbaria, and researchers' files.

ideas.title: Semantic and quality-aware pipelines for mobilizing analysis-ready trait data
ideas.description: This system creates pipelines that prioritize the production of analysis-ready trait data from diverse sources. It integrates automated tools for extracting and validating essential metadata (e.g., method, spatial context, uncertainty) and maps raw data to a core trait ontology upon ingestion. Each record receives quality flags based on provenance and completeness, allowing users to filter data by fitness-for-purpose. The result is not just more data, but a pre-harmonized, quality-documented resource that directly feeds into global synthesis workflows.

ideas.title: Mechanism-Constrained Probabilistic Imputation for Trait Data with Integrated Uncertainty Quantification
ideas.description: This system applies machine learning not as a black box, but as a component within a constrained probabilistic framework that enforces domain knowledge. It combines flexible pattern recognition from neural networks with hard and soft constraints derived from established biophysical principles, ecological trade-offs, and allometric scaling laws. The output is not a single imputed value but a distribution of plausible values, with quantified uncertainty that reflects both data sparsity and potential model conflict. This approach ensures imputations are both data-informed and biologically realistic, making downstream analyses more robust by explicitly accounting for imputation confidence.
